hook: You already have an intuitive sense of functions from algebra — input a number, get a number out. But functions are far more powerful and general than $f(x) = x^2$: they can map sets of any kind, and their structure reveals deep truths about the sizes of infinity itself. How can we rigorously compare whether two infinite sets are the 'same size'?
concept: This unit builds functions from the ground up as precise mathematical objects, then uses them as a lens to classify sets by size — culminating in one of mathematics' most surprising results: not all infinities are equal. The unifying idea is the 'function as structure-preserving map,' formalized through the concept of a bijection.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to precisely define a function and distinguish between its codomain (the declared target set) and its range (the values actually hit), building the rigorous foundation everything else depends on.
Master the three key properties of functions — one-to-one, onto, and both — and learn to prove whether a given function satisfies each property.
Apply function composition to chain mappings together, and determine exactly when a function has an inverse by connecting invertibility to bijectivity.
You will learn to compare the sizes of infinite sets by finding bijections between them, and classify sets as finite, countably infinite, or uncountable.
Explore Cantor's landmark diagonal argument to prove that the real numbers — and any power set — are strictly larger than the natural numbers, shattering the idea that 'infinity is infinity.'
Apply the Schröder-Bernstein theorem to prove that two sets have equal cardinality by constructing injections in both directions, without needing an explicit bijection.
